3 min read
4 hours ago

--

When I first started learning JavaScript, I looked at let x = 10; as just a simple line of code.
It was something I typed without thinking much about what each part actually represented.
As I continue learning JavaScript, Playwright, and QA Automation, I’m trying to understand not only how to write code, but also how JavaScript works behind the scenes.
One of the first things I realized is that even a simple statement like:
let x = 10;contains multiple building blocks, and each one has a specific purpose.
Understanding these fundamentals has completely changed the way I read JavaScript code.
Breaking Down the Statement
Let’s examine each part individually.
let x = 10;1. let — Keyword
let is a JavaScript keyword.
Keywords are reserved words that have predefined meanings in the language and cannot be used as variable names.
In this example, let tells JavaScript that we are declaring a new variable.
Example:
let age = 25;
let city = "Toronto";2. x — Identifier
x is an identifier.
An identifier is simply the name we give to a variable, function, class, or object so we can reference it later.
Examples:
let age = 25;
let firstName = "Harneet";
let isLoggedIn = true;Good identifiers make code easier to read and understand.
3. = — Assignment Operator
The = symbol is the assignment operator.
Its job is to assign the value on the right side to the variable on the left.
let age = 25;This means:
Store the value 25 inside the variable named age.
It is important not to confuse the assignment operator (=) with the equality operators (== and ===).
4. 10 — Numeric Literal
10 is called a numeric literal.
A literal is simply a fixed value written directly in the source code.
Examples of literals include:
10
"Hello"
true
false
nullIn our example:
let x = 10;the value 10 is the numeric literal being assigned to x.
5. ; — Statement Terminator
The semicolon (;) marks the end of a statement.
Although JavaScript can automatically insert semicolons in many situations (Automatic Semicolon Insertion), using them consistently can make your code easier to read and help avoid certain edge cases.
So although it is often optional, many developers still choose to include it.
Visual Breakdown
let x = 10;let → Keyword
x → Identifier
= → Assignment Operator
10 → Numeric Literal
; → Statement TerminatorPress enter or click to view image in full size

Why Does This Matter?
At first glance, this looks like just one line of code.
But understanding what each part does helps build a much stronger foundation for learning JavaScript.
These concepts become increasingly important as you move into topics like:

• Functions

• Objects

• Closures

• Scope

• Hoisting

• Playwright automation

• Debugging JavaScript applications
Every JavaScript program is built using these same building blocks.
My Biggest Takeaway
Before learning this concept, I mostly focused on JavaScript syntax.
Now I understand that every JavaScript statement is made up of several individual components, each with its own responsibility.
Learning these fundamentals has helped me appreciate how JavaScript is structured before the engine even begins executing the code.
As I continue my journey into JavaScript, Playwright, and QA Automation, I’ll keep documenting concepts like these — not only to reinforce my own learning but also to help others who are just getting started.
